coredata.dc:description: The development of easy, translucent, and scalable UV-protection coatings for transparent containers is of utmost importance to ensure the safety of several food and medical products. Here it is studied the preparation of coatings based on carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) with very high absolute emission quantum yields (above 55%) for an excitation wavelenght of 360 nm, and polyvinyl alcohol, using the same temperature but employing two different heating-assisted (conventional and microwave oven-assisted heating) hydrothermal syntheses. The microwave oven-assisted heating allows the formation of the C-QDs in only 1 min, while 180 min are needed to produce them in a conventional oven. The impact of dip-coating withdrawal rates on the coatings properties, such as thickness, UV protective ability and colour is extensively studied. The coatings made under microwave oven-assisted heating achieved superior UV absorbance and colour gain. The best coatings were either the ones prepared under conventional oven for 300 min or the ones synthetised by microwave oven over 15 min, displaying 85% or 92% of UV protection capacity, respectively. The selection of the best conditions strongly depends on the level of colour deemed acceptable, with the microwave oven coatings showing more intense colour gain.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the procedure to make the UV protective coatings.
Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance spectra of colloidal suspensions of C-QDs in water made in the: A) MW for 15 min and B) in the lab oven for 300 min, both diluted in a 1:100 ratio.

Transmission electron micrographs and histograms of: A and C – O300min and B and D – MW15min, respectively. The C-QDs made in the oven are slightly bigger and have a larger size distribution than MW C-QD.

Excitation (left side) and emission (right) fluorescence spectra obtained at different emission detection and excitation wavelengths, for the colloidal water suspensions of O300min (top) and MW15min (bottom) diluted in a 1:1000 and 1:200 ratios, respectively. The dotted lines are the normalization to the excitation (360 nm) and emission (450 nm) maxima. The strong absorption and emission of the samples allows to collect the data under direct excitation light. The asterisk denotes the second order of the 250 nm excitation. The insets show photographs of the blue-emitting C-QDs without and under 365 nm UV lamp with visible room light.

Fluorescence decay curves obtained at different detection wavelengths using the NanoLED-390 as excitation source for the colloidal water suspensions: A – O300min and B – MW15min. The instrumental response function (IRF; black) was measured using a LUDOX ® scattering water solution. The solid lines represent the best fits, obtained with the Horiba DAS6 software.
Zeta potential variation as a function of pH of O300min (red curve) and MW15min (black curve). Optimal dispersion for both is around pH = 10.5.
Shear viscosity as a function of shear rate: A) Oven (O) and B) MW colloidal suspensions.

A and C − Thickness in 3 equidistant zones of the coatings for O300min and MW15min, respectively. B and D − The coatings’ average thickness as a function of the withdrawal rate for coatings O300min and MW15min, respectively.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) transmittance spectra of thecoatings with withdrawal rate from 1 to 100 mm/min, using colloidal suspensions made in the lab oven for: A) 60 min; B) 120 min; C) 180 min; D) 240 min and E) 300 min.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) transmittance spectra of thecoatings with withdrawal rate from 1 to 100 mm/min, using colloidal suspensions made in the microwave oven for A) 1 min; B) 5 min; C) 10 min and D) 15 min.

Percentage of absorbed radiation in the UV range of coatings made at distinct withdrawal rates of coatings deposited using colloidal suspensions made in: A) conventional oven and B) microwave-assisted oven.
Colour difference of the coatings deposited using different withdrawal rates. Coatings deposited using colloidal suspensions made in the: A) conventional and B) microwave ovens.

head.ce:title: Development of carbon quantum dots-based transparent coatings for enhanced UV shielding

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of easy, translucent, and scalable UV-protection coatings for transparent containers is of utmost importance to ensure the safety of several food and medical products. Here it is studied the preparation of coatings based on carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) with very high absolute emission quantum yields (above 55%) for an excitation wavelenght of 360 nm, and polyvinyl alcohol, using the same temperature but employing two different heating-assisted (conventional and microwave oven-assisted heating) hydrothermal syntheses. The microwave oven-assisted heating allows the formation of the C-QDs in only 1 min, while 180 min are needed to produce them in a conventional oven. The impact of dip-coating withdrawal rates on the coatings properties, such as thickness, UV protective ability and colour is extensively studied. The coatings made under microwave oven-assisted heating achieved superior UV absorbance and colour gain. The best coatings were either the ones prepared under conventional oven for 300 min or the ones synthetised by microwave oven over 15 min, displaying 85% or 92% of UV protection capacity, respectively. The selection of the best conditions strongly depends on the level of colour deemed acceptable, with the microwave oven coatings showing more intense colour gain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The UV radiation on the earth's surface constitutes around 5% of all solar radiation. These 5% are composed of UVA (∼95 %), and a small fraction of UVB (∼5%) radiation [1]. Moreover, UV-A and B can negatively impact food and other light-sensitive products such as medicines [2] by compromising their properties. Regarding food, UV radiation can significantly weaken or ruin fatty and oily foods by accelerating their oxidation [3,4], leading to the development of unpleasant rancid sensorial properties [4]. In medicine products, UV radiation can alter the pharmacological properties and physical aspects (e.g., colour) [5,6] decreasing or eradicating their value to consumers. Thus, it is essential to use adequate packaging to protect these product classes.

Typically, to have glass packaging fully capable of protecting their content against UV radiation, UV absorbing species are added to the glass matrix. The result is coloured glass, usually either amber or green [7,8]. Amber allows complete absorption until around 450 nm, while green glass absorbs a significant amount of UV radiation, coinciding with the absorption bands of riboflavin (commonly known as vitamin B2) [9,10]. Riboflavin plays a crucial role in various photochemical reactions that give rise to undesired compounds, such as 3-methyl-2-butene-1-thiol in beer [11]. Nevertheless, packaging designed with intentional coloration obstructs consumers from visually inspecting products such as food or beverages products before purchase [12,13]. Consequently, a preference for transparent packaging arises reflecting consumers' desire to feel secure by observing the unaltered form of the product [12,13]. This has led to a growing demand to develop transparent packages with improved UV absorbing capabilities, protecting the content and satisfying the consumers' preferred aesthetic [14,15].

Another approach to achieve UV protection is through transparent, non-coloured coatings containing UV-absorbing agents. Inorganic semiconductor nanoparticles like ZnO, TiO2, and CeO2 are commonly used due to their bandgap properties (in the UVB and UVA ranges), low cost, non-toxicity, and physicochemical stability [16–18]. However, the development of UV-shielding coatings with full UV-shielding based on this approach still faces some challenges such as colour gain and opacity [19]. Moreover, due to their high surface area, nanoparticles are prone to agglomeration, resulting in sizes that interact with visible light wavelengths and thereby compromising optical transparency [20].

A way to deal with these issues is the use of quantum dots (QDs), particles with a diameter bellow 10 nm. In this size range the quantum dots experience quantum confinement effect, which leads to a collapse of the continuous energy bands of the material into discrete, atomic-like energy levels, direct impacting the bandgap of the material. This means that the bandgap of QDs is tunable by adjusting its size [21,22]. Consequently, the QDs' resulting cut-off allows some flexibility in the UV range, favouring the design of low colour full UV-shielding devices [19].

Recent reports showed that the in-situ precipitation of QDs allows the development of glasses with full UV-shielding. However, the proposed solutions involve the utilization of unconventional glass systems that are applicable to large-scale industrial processing. Furthermore, the thermal processing demanded for nucleation and growth of QDs in glasses are not scalable, due to long processing times (>20 h) [23,24]. On the other hand, coatings or films based on quantum dots present full UV-shielding capacity and very high transmittance on visible spectra, being an outstanding solution.
Many works in this field fail to achieve UV protection beyond 350 nm [25–27] and those that do reach 400 nm often fall short of complete absorption in the UVB region (280–315 nm) [28].

In recent years, carbon-based quantum dots (C-QDs) have garnered significant interest due to their ease of synthesis, non-toxicity, eco-friendliness, photo-stability, and low cost [29,30]. These properties make C-QDs ideal candidates for a wide range of applications, including bioimaging [31,32], drug delivery [32,33] and sensors [34,35]. Importantly, their UV-shielding capabilities make them particularly suitable for developing UV-protective coatings [19]. To capitalize on the use of C-QDs for UV-protective purposes, it is preferable to incorporate them into coatings.

The dip coating technique is a scalable, low-cost, and reproducible method for the industrial coating of packaging materials [30] with polymeric layers containing C-QDs [36,37]. This method involves the deposition of a liquid solution or suspension by immersing the substrate in a bath, with the final film thickness being sensitive to the substrate's withdrawal speed, as described by the Landau-Levich regime [38,39]. However, when the bath is based not only on a liquid, but on a colloidal suspension, the solids loading, and the viscosity of the suspension are key parameters to determine the homogeneity and final thickness of the coating [40]. Inappropriate processing parameters can lead to thick and heterogeneous coatings, compromising the transparency and inducing a colour perceptibility [40,41].
This challenge extends to numerous UV-absorbing films and coatings, where the struggle lies in preserving colourlessness while providing effective protection around 400 nm [23,28].

Consequently, this work focuses on developing an easy and scalable procedure to achieve highly translucent UV protective coatings. For it, C-QDs were synthetized by a conventional oven (O)- or a microwave (MW)-assisted hydrothermal method. In the conventional oven method, the hydrothermal synthesis occurred gradually, allowing for precise temperature regulation and extended reaction times. Conversely, the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method expedited synthesis by utilizing microwave irradiation, facilitating rapid and uniform heating. This dual approach offered flexibility in tailoring synthesis conditions to achieve specific characteristics desired for the intended application.

Moreover, in the optimization process, colour, as a crucial parameter, was identified as adjustable to meet acceptable colour gain criteria. This adjustment was implemented during the deposition of coatings based on PVA and containing C-QDs onto the top of glass plates, achieved through dip coating at varying withdraw speeds. The subsequent results are discussed comprehensively based on the homogeneity, colour, and UV-shielding capacity promoted by the coatings.

PVA/C-QD colloidal suspensions were produced using two distinct heating methods (conventional oven- and MW-assisted). Different deposition rates via dip-coating were examined to identify the optimal coating, considering factors such as colour and UV absorption capabilities.

Citric acid (CA, >99.5 %, Merck), branched polyethylenimine (β-PEI, average MW ∼ 800 by LS, average Mn ∼ 600 by GPC, Sigma-Aldrich), Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, 98 % Merck, MW 72000), distilled water. Glass slides used D100001 (Delta Lab).

The synthesized UV absorbing coatings were adapted from Hess et al. [42] and modified to by utilizing two different procedures. One used conventional heating in a lab oven with a stain steel autoclave with a Teflon liner to create the necessary pressure for the hydrothermal synthesis (O colloidal suspension), and the other used a microwave synthesis oven (MW samples). The microwave used was a CEM Discovery-SP, the experiment was performed in a closed vessel with the same volume as in the oven 20 ml, with low stirring.

The coatings were made using water-based colloidal suspensions with 10 wt% PVA, 2 wt% of β-PEI, and 10 wt% of CA. The colloidal suspensions were then heated using either the conventional oven (60 min to 300 min) or the microwave oven (1 min to 15 min) at 135 °C. The MW colloidal suspension also underwent a heating ramp under 8 min to reach the 135 °C. The O samples were only placed in the oven (Memmert model une 400) pre-heated at 135 °C – as seen in Fig. 1 .

The deposition of the colloidal suspensions onto glass substrates is crucial since the rates used will directly impact the adhesiveness and thickness of the coatings. Immersion rates used for all coatings were 50 mm/min, while withdrawal rates varied from 1 to 100 mm/min using a KSV model DC dip-coater.

The code for the coatings is in the format xy_z. The x corresponds to the type of heating treatment used: “O” for conventional oven with an autoclave or “MW” for samples that used microwave-assisted synthesis. y corresponds to the times used (in min). Lastly, z denotes the withdrawal rate used in deposition of the sample. For example, the coating made by thermal treatment in a conventional oven with heating treatment of 300 min, deposited with a withdrawal rate of 25 mm/min, is called: O300min_25.

The UV–Vis spectroscopy used a Shimadzu UV LISR-3100 (JP) spectrometer, using water as background reference material, with range 250 – 700 nm and 1 nm step, in absorbance mode. The colloidal suspensions rheological properties were characterized using a rotational rheometer (Kinexus Pro+, Netzsch, Selb, Germany).
The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was performed over the range of 4000 – 400 cm−1 with Perkin Elmer Spectrum BX with a 2 cm−1 resolution, with 1 cm−1 intervals and 256 scans.

Raman spectra were measured using a Raman Spectrometer Wasatch 532 ER, coupled to an Olympus BH2 microscope with a 50x objective and NA = 0.45 (spot size ∼ 15 µm). The excitation with a Cobolt laser at the 532 nm line. The acquisition was performed using a selected laser power of 10 mW. Spectra were acquired with an integration time of 1 s with 10 accumulations each. The backgrounds were removed, and the spectra were normalized using the water band.
Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were acquired with a Phillips X’Pert MPD diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.54178 Å.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was used to reveal the surface of the coatings. The SEM equipment used was Hitachi® model SU-70. Glass coated samples (O300min_25 and MW15min_25) were fixed on SEM specimen holders using double-sided carbon tape. A conductive carbon thin film was then deposited onto the films using a carbon rod coater (Emitech K950X). The images were taken with magnifications at 2000x times at 8 kV.

Local topographies of the samples deposited in glass (O300min_25 and MW15min_25) were studied using a commercial Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) (Ntegra NTMDT) in conventional semi-contact AFM mode. The SPARK 150Pt (NuNano) probes (resonant frequency 150 kHz, force constant 18 N/m) were utilized. The measurements were performed under controlled conditions (30 °C, humidity of about 40%).

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of the synthesized C-QDs were obtained using a high-resolution 200 kV filtered power transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, model 2200FS, Tokyo, Japan). The colloidal suspensions were diluted in a factor of 1:100000 (v/v) and filtered through a Whatman cellulose acetate with glass microfiber membrane syringe filter (0.2 µm). A droplet of this suspension was deposited onto a holey carbon coated copper grid from Agar Scientific® and left to evaporate in an oven at 60 °C. Measurements were taken for more than 50 C-QDs to provide a comprehensive understanding of their size and size dispersion.

Photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra were recorded using a Fluorolog3® Horiba Scientific (Model FL3-22) spectroscope, with a modular double grating excitation spectrometer (fitted with a 1200 grooves/mm grating blazed at 330 nm) and a TRIAX 320 single emission monochromator (fitted with a 1200 grooves/mm grating blazed at 500 nm, reciprocal linear density of 2.6 nm mm−1), coupled to a R928 Hamamatsu photomultiplier. The excitation source was a 450 W Xe arc lamp. All the spectra (excitation and emission) were collected with the same experimental setup: right-angle acquisition mode and the excitation and emission slits aperture fixed at a bandwidth of 1.4 nm. The emission spectra were corrected for detection and optical spectral response of the spectrofluorometer, and the excitation spectra were corrected for the spectral distribution of the lamp intensity using a photodiode reference detector. The ns emission decay curves were acquired with the spectrofluorometer coupled to a Single Proton Counting Controler Fluorohub (Horiba Scientific) working at 950 V, and using a pulsed NanoLED-390 (Horiba Scientific) as the excitation source. The instrumental response function for the NanoLED-390 was obtained using a LUDOX® scattering water solution.

The absolute emission quantum yields (q) were measured at room temperature (298 K) using a Quantaurus quantum yield measurement system (QY Plus C13534, Hamamatsu) with a 150 W Xenon lamp coupled to a monochromator for wavelength discrimination, an integrating sphere as sample chamber, and two multi-channel analysers for signal detection in the visible spectral range. The values of q for the C-QD water suspensions emission correspond to the emission integration over the 390 − 750 nm spectral range under 360 nm excitation. Distilled water was used as the reference. The reported values present an accuracy of 10%, according to the manufacturer. However, in the three consecutive measurements recorded for each sample it was observed an accuracy better than 0.5%.

The zeta potential was measured with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, United Kingdom). Each coating was measured in 3 runs with 50 scans each. With a previous 60 s of stabilizing time. The pH values were adjusted with various sodium hydroxide (NaOH) molar solutions (0.01 M to 1 M).

Viscosity versus shear rate measurements were evaluated in the shear rate range of 0.1 to 100 s−1, using a cone–plate system (4°/40 mm) with 1500 µm gap. All measurements were assessed at room temperature (25 °C), holding a solvent trap to prevent coatings drying during experiments. The colour of the coatings was determined using a Colour Meter CIELab (Konika Minolta CR 400). The colour difference (ΔE), Eq. (1), in the CIE L*a*b* colour space was calculated with the formula and represents the colour gain provided solely by a given coating [43]:

Moreover, the coatings’ thickness and local topography was studied using a Sensofar Model S-Neox profilometer. The data were obtained with a 10 × interferometric objective (acquisition area of 1.75 mm × 1.35 mm) using the vertical shiftinterferometry technique (VSI).

C-QDs were synthetised using conventional using conventional oven (O) and microwave (MW) methods for different durations. Fig. 2 showcases the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance spectra of water-diluted colloidal suspensions of PVA/C-QD, highlighting the synthesis performed using conventional oven (O) and microwave (MW) methods for durations of 300 and 15 min, respectively. Notably, both MW_15min and O_300min suspensions exhibit the ability to absorb radiation across the entire UV range (250–400 nm). Upon careful analysis of the absorbance in colloidal suspensions diluted at a 1:100 ratio, MW_15min demonstrates a peak absorption at 363 nm, while O_300min exhibits a peak at 358 nm.

The absorption between 300 and 400 nm can, in both colloidal suspensions, be assigned to the n → π* transition of the C = O bond in the C-QD carbon cores. [44] The presence of these C = O is supported by the FTIR data in Fig. S1
(
Supplementary Material
− SM), which show the corresponding bands at 1632 cm−1. [45] Other relevant bands include 3380 cm−1 suggesting the presence of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [46] and at 1229 cm−1 was related to H–C–H bending mode in –CH3 species in the PVA matrix. [47] Furthermore, MW15min absorbance spectrum is broader than the one of O300min. These factors contribute to MW15min absorbing more radiation in the visible range leading to higher colour gain in this coating than O300min. It is possible to observe as well that the colloidal suspension MW15min has a lower radiation absorbance than O300min (Fig. 2). This diminished absorbance capacity is due to a lower concentration of C-QDs in MW15min than in O300min. This difference in concentration is likely due the longer reaction time in the oven synthesis which allows for more extensive reaction completion, potentially leading to a higher yield of C-QDs. Since partial absorption occurs in both colloidal suspensions beyond the 400 nm threshold extending into the visible spectrum, particularly within the range of purple and blue wavelengths (390–––495 nm), the corresponding complementary colours of orange and yellow, respectively, become observable to the naked eye. [28].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis further confirmed the crystalline nature of the C-QDs, revealing characteristic peaks corresponding to the graphite-like structure of the carbon quantum dots, including a band around 2θ 18° − Fig. S2
(SM), corresponding to (002) hkl plane [31,48] This peak indicates that the interlayer spacing (d) of the C-QDs is approximately 0.25 nm.[31,49] Raman spectra were collected using water diluted suspensions of the C-QDs to reduce the fluorescence signal interference and are displayed in Fig. S3
(SM). As the initial colloidal suspensions were diluted 200x and 1000x, the characteristic water vibration bands are very pronounced. [50] Still the typical D and G carbon bands, assigned to vibrations of carbon atoms due to the presence of structural defects and in-plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, respectively, can be observed at ca. 1415 cm-1 and 1550 cm-1 (insets of Fig. S3 ).[51].

The size and morphology of the C-QDs are crucial parameters that influence the coating properties. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) provided an overview of the surface morphology ( Fig. S4
, SM), revealing a mostly homogeneous surface. The local scan of the surfaces using the AFM technique reveals the topography features contributing directly to the roughness of the samples. At the scan of 5 x 5 µm, the appearance of large globular structures for both samples was detected ( Fig. S5
, SM). For the sample O300min_25, the average diameter of a globular structure is measured at about 4 µm ( Fig. S5
, a and b), and for the sample MW15min_25 is about 1 µm ( Fig. S5
, c and d). The average roughness for both samples at this scan dimension can be estimated at the value of about 100 nm. Within the local AFM scan, there are plane areas where the smallest globular structures can be identified. These points of interest were discovered in both samples and are marked by dashed squares in Fig. S5
, c and d. A very local AFM scan of these areas at 1.5 x 1.5 µm displays the appearance of globular structures with the average roughness for both samples at about 15 nm (inserts in Fig. S5
, a and c). Additionally, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis in Fig. 3 , was performed to visualize the C-QDs morphology and measure their sizes. As seen in Fig. 3, both the C-QDs made in the in the conventional oven (A) and MW (B) have round shapes.

Their sizes vary slightly with oven C-QDs 5.98 ± 1.28 nm and MW C-QDs measuring an average 4.51 ± 0.88 nm. In Fig. 3 C, the inset reveals the size distribution of the C-QDs synthesized in the oven, ranging from 3.5 to 8.5 nm. Conversely, in Fig. 3 D, the histogram illustrates that C-QDs synthesized via microwave (MW) exhibit a more confined size distribution, ranging from 2.5 to 6.5 nm. This points to the increase synthesis time potentially allowing some further growth of the C-QDs.

Because C-QDs are excellent fluorescent nanomaterials, the photoluminescence properties of MW_15min and O_300min colloidal water suspensions were evaluated. Due to the strong absorption of the colloidal suspensions, dilution ratios of 1:200 and 1:1000, for MW_15min and O_300min, respectively, were needed to ensure that, the excitation light at their maximum of absorption (ca. 360 nm) pass through all fluorescence cell (1 cm thick), preventing the inner filter effect.

Fig. 4 presents the excitation and emission spectra of MW_15min and O_300min in water suspensions, recorded for different detection and excitation wavelengths, respectively. Except for the intensity, which is 1.4 times greater for O_300min, these spectra are almost identical for the two C-QD suspensions. The outstanding performance of the C-QD fluorescence are illustrated by the low intensity of the direct excitation light detected and by the photographs showing intense blue-light under 365 nm UV lamp even with visible room light.

For both C-QD suspensions the excitation spectra, detected at the maximum of the emission (450 nm), display a main broad band between 280 and 415 nm in the UVB and UVA spectral regions, with the maximum peaking at 360 nm, and another in the UVC region, below 270 nm were the intensity of the Xe excitation lamp is very low. Excitation bands in the UVB and UVA regions are usually reported for C-QDs and commonly attributed to bandgap transitions of conjugated π-domains, surface defect states, or to small fluorescent molecules or fluorophores connected on the surface or into the core of the carbon backbone.[52,53] The excitation bands in the UVC region are rarely reported for C-QDs.[54] However, they appear frequently in the excitation spectra of organic fluorophores originating from the upper excited states. The change on the emission detection from 425 to 540 nm induces a small red shift of the 280–415 nm band as unveil by the normalized excitation lines but, the UVC excitation band stays constant.

The MW_15min and O_300min emission spectra excited at 360 nm (Fig. 4) is characterized by a broad band spanning from the violet (395 nm) to the red (600 nm) visible spectral regions with their maximum intensity at 450 nm. Despite that, the emission is perceived as blue light as demonstrated by photographs inserted in Fig. 4. On the one hand, the emission band profile stays unaffected when the excitation wavelength was changed to the higher excited state at 250 nm. On the other hand, by changing the excitation wavelength to the low energy region induces a red shift of the band, up to 22 nm for the 410 nm excitation, relatively to the 360 nm excitation. The observed red shifts of the emission band with the excitation change are consistent with the C-QD particle size distributions observed by TEM analysis (Fig. 3 ).

The decay curves of O_300min and MW_15min water suspension excited at 390 nm and detecting the emission at 425, 450, 475 and 540 nm are presented in Fig. 5 . All decay curves were best fitted with the second order exponential function presented in Equation (2).

is the intensity, t is time, α is the percentage of relative amplitude of decay, and τ stands for the lifetime.

The absolute emission quantum yield of the MW_15min and O_300min colloidal water suspensions are high, with values of 56.6% and 55.4%, respectively for an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, clear evidence for the high efficiency of the C-QD by the two hydrothermal syntheses procedures, conventional oven- and microwave oven-assisted heating. Concerning blue-emitting C-QD the high reported emission quantum yields values are only surpassed by three reported N doped (74%) and N and S co-doped C-QD (69% and 75.6%).[54–56].

To further enhance understanding of the colloidal suspensions, the zeta potential variation at different pH levels was analysed to ensure optimal dispersion and improved absorbance capacity, the results shown in Fig. 6
.

The MW15min colloidal suspension exhibits an isoelectric point near a pH value of 3.5 while O300min is more positively charged, resulting in increased electrostatic repulsion of individual particles. The dielectric point of O300min is most likely under pH 2, where a larger volume of added acid species (usually HCl) is needed to reach it. Thus, this means that O300min is more stable than MW15min. For both, the absolute zeta potential tends to increase with the increase of the pH values reaching the maximum value at around pH 10.5. The highest absolute zeta potential of MW15min is 43 mV and for O300min is 50 mV. At this pH value both are considered good stable colloidal suspensions.[57].

Indeed, an absolute zeta potential value above 30 mV is generally considered to have sufficient repulsive force to attain better physical colloidal stability.[57] Smaller zeta potential values can lead to C-QD aggregation, due to the van der Waals attractive forces which act upon them.[57] Moreover, the aggregation decreases the absorbance of the C-QD in the region of their band gap (UV range) suffering a red-shift.[58] As a result, the absorbance in the visible range increases leading to the coatings’ undesirable colour gain.

Furthermore, to achieve a coating that is as colourless as possible, besides avoiding agglomeration in the colloidal suspension, it is also paramount to control the coatings’ thickness. If a coating is too thick, it will become yellow. The most influential parameters to ensure the coatings homogeneity and thickness are the colloidal suspensions viscosity and the withdrawal rate used during the dip-coating process. [40,41].

Consequently, studies were conducted to analyse the viscosity, specifically shear viscosity, of all the colloidal suspensions prepared both in the oven (at intervals of 60, 120, 180, 240, and 300 min) and using the MW method (for durations of 1, 5, 10, and 15 min). The shear viscosity profiles of these colloidal suspension, plotted as a function of the shear rate, are presented in Fig. 7 .

All colloidal suspensions behave as Newtonian fluids i.e., display constant viscosity with increasing shear rates between 0.1 and 100 s−1, which contains the region of interest 10–100 s−1. The more relevant region is between the values of 10–100 s−1 because these are the typical shear rates ranges for the dip-coating process. [59] This Newtonian flow behaviour in similar to the one reported by Gao et al. [60] that also worked with high concentration PVA solutions in water. Their work focused on exploring the rheological characteristics of PVA solutions with varying degrees of polymerization. The PVA used, in this work, is of high molar weight at 72000 Da, thus it likely provides steric stabilization in the colloidal suspensions.[61] Furthermore, as the heated colloidal suspensions exhibit reduced viscosity, an extended heating period leads to a further decline in viscosity. Between both assisted heating methods, MW shows the lowest viscosity and highest viscosity loss with heating (Fig. 7). According to literature, this is because MW radiation can degrade the PVA polymer and it is able to do it significantly faster than conventional oven heating. [62,63] This PVA degradation can also lead to some loss of the steric stabilization allowing the agglomeration of the C-QDs. And as mentioned before, the aggregation leads to a red-shift and coatings with enhanced yellow colouring. Thus, coatings made with MW colloidal suspensions have the highest colour gain.

The difference in viscosity will also impact the thickness of the coatings achieved via the dip-coating. Consequently, we have studied the effect of the synthesis route on suspensions viscosity and its impact on the coatings’ thickness.

To understand the impact of the viscosity and withdrawal rate on the coatings’ homogeneity and thickness, the coating cross-sections were measured at three distinct equidistant points (scheme in SM,
Fig. S6 ) and are presented in Fig. 8 .

In Fig. 8 A, the three distinct zones measured show variations in their thickness. Notably, zone 1, as depicted in both Fig. 8 A and C, displays a significant thickness increase compared to zones 2 and 3, adhering to the pattern 1 > 2 > 3. This also indicates pooling taking place in zone 1 particularly at high extraction rates. Furthermore, there is a clear correlation between an increase in extraction rate and heightened thickness, as observed in both Fig. 8 B and D. Coatings produced using the oven colloidal suspension (O) consistently exhibited greater thickness compared to those made with microwave (MW) colloidal suspensions across all extraction rates ranging from 1 to 50 mm/s. This distinction is evident in Fig. 8 A, where coatings consistently show greater thickness at equivalent rates compared to those in Fig. 8 C.

The Landau − Levich model describes the relationship between the wet film thickness and the withdrawal rate of the substrate (when surface tension is considered). [64] This model conceives 3 distinct regions. [65] The first at lower rates is typically the capillary region where the films’ thickness decreases with the increase in withdrawal rate. Around the rates where minimum thickness is achieved and thickness varies little, is the second regime – the intermediate region. The third region is the draining region. In this region the films become thicker when increasing withdrawal rate. The behaviour observed in Fig. 8 A and C deviates from the results expected from the use of the Landau − Levich model. In our case the coatings behave similarly to the work of Berteloot et al.. [40] This similarity is due to the fact that both works use colloid suspensions. The rates in our work, 1 mm/min to 100 mm/min (or 1.6 x 10-5 m/s to 1.6 x 10-3 m/s), are considered low to intermediate by Berteloot et al. and in these regimes the coatings thickness increases with the withdrawal rate. The primary factors affecting film thickness at these rates are i) evaporation and ii) the hydrodynamic field that the evaporation creates. At the contact line is where most evaporation takes place. Besides the increase in thickness with the withdrawal rate, it can also be observed that the coatings made from O300min are thicker than the ones made from MW15min (Fig. 8 B and D, respectively) at the same rates used in the dip-coating process. This difference in due to the difference in the suspension viscosity (Fig. 7).

As previously mentioned, the viscosity comparison reveals that O300min is more viscous than MW15min. This implies that at the contact line, the O300min colloidal suspension contains a higher concentration of PVA molecules compared to MW15min. The higher viscosity of O300min indicates less polymer degradation during heating. Consequently, more PVA with carbon quantum dots (C-QD) dries at the contact line, leading to thicker coatings for the same drying rates and process time.
The relationship between thickness and withdrawal rate in all other coatings, made via conventional oven and MW are in Figs. S7 and S8 (SM), respectively.

The values in Fig. 8 A and C show the average values at a given rate, as well as the standard deviation of all measured coatings. However, for optimal analysis of coating homogeneity, Fig. 8 B and D provide the most suitable insights. Big differences between these 3 zones point to high thickness variability throughout the coating. As seen in Fig. 8 B and D higher rates result in not only higher thickness but also thickness variation among the measured zones. This occurs because, at the highest rates, pooling typically happens at the bottom of the glass slide, as illustrated in zone 1 – Fig. 8 B and D. All the measurements in the 3 zones for all the different coatings (oven 300 min, 240 min, 180 min, 120 min and 60 min and MW 1 min, 5 min, 10 min and 15 min) are shown in Figs. S9 and S10 (SM), respectively. Consequently, to have a coating with homogenous thickness throughout, the withdrawal rate should be kept at around 10 mm/min for the samples made from O300 and 25 mm/min for samples made with MW15min.

As observed the withdrawal rate used in the dip-coating process directly inform the thickness of the coatings. Moreover, increasing the thickness of the coatings will result in increasing UV absorbing capabilities since the UV rays will contact to a higher amount of C-QDs. Simultaneously if visible radiation is also absorbed this will result in an added colour gain. Hence it is relevant to know the effect of withdrawal rate of the dip-coating process on the coatings’ absorption ability and colour.

Fig. 9 presents ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) transmittance spectra of the coatings with withdrawal rates from 1 to 100 mm/min, using colloidal suspensions made in the conventional oven at 135 °C, for 60 min, 120 min, 180 min, 240 min and 300 min.

As seen in Fig. 9, coatings with colloidal suspension O_60min do not absorb radiation. This is because the C-QDs have not formed; thus, there are effectively no absorbing species present. 60 min reaction is not enough time to create the C-QDs under conventional oven heating. In the coatings made with colloidal suspension O_120min, the C-QDs are starting to form, and a portion of radiation is absorbed by the PVA due to increased turbidity. This turbidity can be observed in the small decreases in transmittance, in the visible range, from approx. 90% (uncoated glass) to under 65% in all O120min coatings. At 180 min, the C-QDs continue to form, and here we can better see the trend that will prevail from this time forward – the coatings made with higher withdrawal rates result in thicker coatings and, therefore, present less transmittance i.e. absorb more radiation. The coatings made with O_240min and O_300min both absorb almost over 90% of UV radiation at higher withdrawal rates (50 and 100 mm/min). Still, the O_300min suspension is the best to coat the glass, absorbing most of the radiation. The best absorbing coatings made from this suspension used 50 and 100 mm/min as withdrawal rates. However as seen in Fig. 8 , these two coatings are too heterogeneous, thus if homogeneity of the coatings’ thickness is important the best rates to be used are below 10 mm/min. Consequently, the best coating is O_300min_10 which absorbs 85% of radiation in UV range (250–400 nm).

Concerning the coatings derived from colloidal suspensions synthesized in the microwave (Fig. 10), they display uniform results, indicating that variations in synthesis times from 1 to 15 min do not yield a significant difference, in contrast to the observed effects in oven-synthesized colloidal suspensions. As seen in Fig. 8 and
Fig. S7 , fast withdrawal rates in MW coatings, result in not only increased thickness coatings but also in its increased heterogeneity. Thus, a limiting rate at or below 25 mm/min is ideal for the set of coatings using the colloidal suspension MW15min.

In Fig. 10 it is difficult to discern which coatings made using 25 mm/min rate absorbs more UV radiation. For that purpose, an analysis of Fig. 11
B is needed.

Fig. 11 displays the percentage of UV radiation absorbed by the different colloidal suspensions and deposited using different withdrawal rates. In Fig. 11 A, the impact of synthesis times (60 to 300 min) on the percentage of absorbed radiation is pronounced, indicating poorer absorption capacity at lower times. Hydrothermal treatments lasting under 180 min result in an absorbance capacity below 70%, and no significant absorption is observed with just 60 min of hydrothermal treatment. Notably, an extension in oven synthesis time consistently enhances absorption capacity. Furthermore, variations in withdrawal rates for the same suspension reveal an impact on absorption capacity, with higher rates yielding a greater percentage of radiation absorption. For instance, the O300min colloidal suspension absorbed only 70% when deposited at 1 mm/min but surpassed 95% with a withdrawal rate of 100 mm/min. In Fig. 11 B, the influence of synthesis time on the colloidal suspensions in the microwave is negligible, with the variance in absorption percentage primarily dependent on the withdrawal rate employed. Once again, higher withdrawal rates demonstrate a consistent pattern of increased absorption, as seen in Fig. 11 A.
Amidst these observations, the MW15min_25 sample stands out in Fig. 11 B, exhibiting a UV absorbance of 92.2% and establishing itself as the most optimal coating under a 25 mm/min withdrawal rate.

Notably, both synthesis time and withdrawal rate play crucial roles, leading to distinct outcomes and absorbing capacities in coatings made from oven colloidal suspensions. By controlling these parameters, diverse coatings can be achieved. In contrast, MW colloidal suspensions show minimal variation among themselves. Therefore, achieving distinct absorbance values in MW colloidal suspensions involves modifying the withdrawal rate, directly influencing the coatings' thickness.

With the results analysed thus far it has been established that the best coatings are O300min_10 and the MW15min_25, with 85% and 92% absorbance in the UV range, respectively. But this is without considering the colour intensity of the coatings. Considering the ΔE, the sample MW15min_25 might be regarded as too yellow. In that case, O300min_10 is the best alternative.

Coatings with UV protection exceeding 90% can be achieved by utilizing colloidal suspensions containing C-QDs produced through either microwave synthesis (shorter durations) or hydrothermal treatment in a conventional oven (longer durations). The microwave-generated coatings exhibit greater uniformity in both absorbance and colour compared to the oven-generated coatings. This uniformity can be attributed to the similarities in the colloidal suspensions produced with distinct synthesis periods, ranging from 1 to 15 min. Therefore, the more impactful and differential parameter for these samples is the withdrawal rate used in the dip-coatings process. Adversely the oven coatings can vary a lot among each other both from the impact of synthesis times (60 to 300 min) as well as the withdrawal rates employed. Notably, C-QDs also take a lot longer to be made (over 180 min) in the lab oven process.

To achieve homogeneity in the coatings thickness and appearance the withdrawal rate used in the dip-coating process must be kept under 25 or even 10 mm/min, depending on the colloidal suspension used. Moreover, since it is undesirable to have yellow coatings on top of the glass substrates, it is important to limit the colour gain provided by the coatings. This colour limitation reduces the appeal of most microwave colloidal suspension-based coatings. Therefore, considering all these factors, the optimal colloidal suspension is the coating produced through hydrothermal treatment for 300 min using a dip-coating procedure with a withdrawal rate of 10 mm/min – denoted as sample O300min_10.
